title: Natural dye mordant overview
subtitle: How mordants work, which are used in natural dyeing, and how each affects dye colour and fibre
excerpt: A mordant is a substance that forms a chemical bridge between a dye molecule and a textile fibre, improving dye uptake, wash-fastness, and light-fastness. Without a mordant, most natural dyes wash out quickly or fade in light. The choice of mordant also shifts the colour: alum produces clean, clear tones; iron saddens and deepens; copper greens; tannin adds a warm grey-brown base on cellulose fibres. This reading covers the main mordants used in natural dyeing and their properties.
sourceNotes: Mordant chemistry and properties are documented throughout natural dye literature. Summaries here are consistent with contemporary guild instruction and Hummel's 'Dyeing of Textile Fabrics' (1896), public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt or plant tannin that forms a chemical bridge between a dye molecule and a textile fibre. Mordants improve dye uptake, wash-fastness, and light-fastness. The mordant also modifies the resulting colour.

Treating fibre with a mordant solution before dyeing. Pre-mordanting is the standard approach in natural dyeing: the mordant is applied first in a separate bath, then the mordanted fibre is transferred to the dye bath.

content.text: is the standard approach: the mordant is applied in a separate bath before dyeing, so the fibre is already prepared when it enters the dye bath.

Alum is the most widely used mordant and the recommended starting point. It produces clear, true colours without significantly shifting the dye hue. Standard quantity is 15-20 g per 100 g of dry fibre. Alum is used at 80-85 degrees Celsius, held for 45-60 minutes. It is the safest mordant in common use: mildly irritating to skin in solution but not acutely toxic. Excess alum (above 20 g per 100 g fibre) can make wool sticky.

Iron saddens colours: yellows shift toward khaki and olive, greens deepen, oranges move toward brown. It is typically used at 4-6 g per 100 g fibre and at lower temperatures (60-70 degrees Celsius) to reduce its harshening effect on wool fibres. Iron in high concentration or at high temperature degrades wool. It is most often used as an after-bath modifier rather than a primary mordant.

Copper greens and olivens dye colours: yellows shift toward olive, greens deepen and cool toward teal. It is typically used at 2-5 g per 100 g fibre as an after-bath modifier. Copper sulphate is toxic to aquatic organisms and soil invertebrates: spent copper baths must be taken to a household chemical disposal point, not poured down the drain or onto soil. Wear rubber gloves throughout.

Tannin is a plant-derived mordant used primarily on cellulose fibres (cotton, linen, hemp) which do not bond well to metallic mordants without a tannin pre-treatment. Oak galls, black tea, and sumac are all high-tannin sources. The tannin bath precedes the metallic mordant bath in the sequence. Tannin itself adds a warm beige-grey base colour to the fibre before dyeing. On wool, tannin is not required but can be used to deepen the ground colour.

Wool, silk, and other protein fibres mordant readily with metallic salts and do not require tannin pre-treatment. Cotton, linen, and other cellulose fibres need a tannin pre-mordant step before metallic mordanting, as their surface chemistry does not bond metallic salts directly. The sequence for cellulose fibres is: scour, tannin bath, metallic mordant bath, dye bath.
